notes.learner_information_genmentor: Mirrors GenMentor's real onboarding: occupation_label + background_text (from onboarding.py:149). Occupation is the required dropdown seed; no FSLSM.
notes.learner_information_enhanced: Mirrors enhanced onboarding: persona label + persona description (from PERSONAS dict in main.py) + background_text. FSLSM dimensions are stored in the learner profile object, not serialized into this string. Persona selection is required; no free-text field.
notes.learner_information: Plain background text only — used as context in judge prompts to avoid polluting the evaluation with system-specific prefixes.
notes.vague_goal_refinement: Scenarios S10–S12 use the deliberately vague learning goal 'I want to learn project management' to highlight a key system difference: the Enhanced system auto-refines underspecified goals into structured, actionable objectives (captured in 'goal_refined_enhanced') before generating a learning plan, whereas GenMentor forwards the vague goal as-is, which typically results in a generic or unfocused plan and requires the learner or instructor to manually clarify scope before a useful learning path can be produced. This distinction is surfaced by the 'goal_is_vague' and 'goal_refinement_note' fields on each affected scenario.

learning_goals.G1: I want to learn full-stack web development using React and Node.js
learning_goals.G2: I want to master machine learning fundamentals and apply them to real datasets
learning_goals.G3: I want to build and deploy a REST API to the cloud
learning_goals.G4: I want to learn project management

B1.label: Complete beginner
B1.text: I am a first-year computer science undergraduate student with no prior programming experience. I have never written code before and am starting completely from scratch. I am motivated and have about 10 hours per week to dedicate to learning.

B2.label: Intermediate
B2.text: I have about 1-2 years of self-taught Python experience. I have built a few small personal projects like a web scraper and a simple calculator app. I do not have a formal computer science degree. I understand basic programming concepts like loops, functions, and data structures, but I have not worked with professional software development practices or large codebases.

B3.label: Career switcher
B3.text: I have 5 years of professional experience in marketing and digital analytics. I am comfortable with Excel, data analysis, and have taught myself basic HTML and CSS through online tutorials. I am highly motivated to transition into software engineering as a career change. I can dedicate 15 hours per week to learning.

B4.label: Junior professional beginner
B4.text: I recently graduated with a business administration degree and have just started my first full-time role as a junior business analyst. I have no formal project management experience and have never used tools like Jira, Trello, or MS Project. I am eager to grow into a project coordinator role and have about 8 hours per week to dedicate to learning.

B5.label: Experienced contributor seeking formal PM skills
B5.text: I have 3 years of experience as a software developer and have informally led small feature teams. I am comfortable collaborating on tasks and managing my own workload, but I have never formally managed a project, created a project charter, or applied structured methodologies like Agile or PRINCE2. I can dedicate 10 hours per week to learning.

B6.label: Technical specialist transitioning to PM
B6.text: I have 7 years of experience as a systems engineer designing and maintaining enterprise infrastructure. I am highly organized and technically strong, but I am now transitioning into a project management role and need to build formal PM skills in areas like stakeholder management, risk planning, budgeting, and scheduling. I can dedicate 12 hours per week to learning.

scenarios.goal_refinement_note: Enhanced system auto-refines 'I want to learn project management' into the structured objective stored in 'goal_refined_enhanced' before generating the learning plan. GenMentor forwards the vague goal as-is, producing a generic or unfocused plan; a useful learning path requires the learner or instructor to manually scope the goal first.
scenarios.learning_goal: I want to learn project management
scenarios.goal_refined_enhanced: I want to develop practical project management skills, covering the full project lifecycle (initiation, planning, execution, monitoring, and closure), key methodologies such as Agile and Waterfall, and essential competencies in stakeholder management, risk assessment, and scheduling, so that I can effectively lead or coordinate projects in a professional setting.

G1: State management in React with Context API
G2: Supervised learning: linear regression and classification
G3: HTTP methods and REST API conventions
Deploying a Node.js app to cloud with Docker
G4: Project lifecycle phases: initiation, planning, execution, monitoring, and closure
Agile and Scrum: sprints, ceremonies, and backlog management
Stakeholder management, risk assessment, and project scheduling tools

rag_ground_truths.G1_React component lifecycle and hooks: React hooks include useState for local state, useEffect for side effects, useContext for context consumption, and useRef for DOM references. The component lifecycle phases are mounting, updating, and unmounting. useEffect with an empty dependency array runs only once on mount. Cleanup functions returned from useEffect run on unmount.
rag_ground_truths.G1_RESTful API design with Express.js: REST APIs use HTTP methods: GET (read), POST (create), PUT/PATCH (update), DELETE (remove). Express.js uses app.get(), app.post() etc. to define routes. Middleware functions process requests before handlers. Status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found, 500 Internal Server Error.
rag_ground_truths.G1_State management in React with Contex: React Context API uses React.createContext(), a Provider component to supply values, and useContext() hook to consume them. It avoids prop drilling. For complex state, useReducer is paired with Context. Redux is an alternative for larger applications with actions, reducers, and a single store.
rag_ground_truths.G2_Supervised learning: linear regressio: Supervised learning trains on labeled data. Linear regression predicts continuous outputs using a best-fit line minimizing mean squared error. Logistic regression and SVMs handle binary classification. Decision trees and random forests handle multi-class classification. Key concepts: hypothesis function, cost function, gradient descent optimization.
rag_ground_truths.G2_Feature engineering and data preproce: Feature engineering includes normalization (min-max scaling), standardization (z-score), one-hot encoding for categoricals, and handling missing values via imputation or deletion. Feature selection methods include correlation analysis, mutual information, and recursive feature elimination.
rag_ground_truths.G2_Model evaluation: cross-validation an: Cross-validation splits data into k folds; train on k-1, test on 1, repeat k times. Metrics for classification: accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC. Metrics for regression: MAE, MSE, RMSE, R-squared. Overfitting is detected when training accuracy >> validation accuracy.
rag_ground_truths.G3_HTTP methods and REST API conventions: REST uses stateless HTTP. GET retrieves resources, POST creates, PUT replaces, PATCH partially updates, DELETE removes. Resources are nouns in URLs (e.g., /users/123). Versioning uses /v1/ prefix. JSON is the standard response format. Status codes 2xx success, 4xx client errors, 5xx server errors.
rag_ground_truths.G3_Authentication and authorization with: JWT (JSON Web Token) consists of header, payload, and signature, base64-encoded and dot-separated. The server signs tokens with a secret key (HMAC) or private key (RSA). Clients send tokens in the Authorization: Bearer header. Access tokens are short-lived; refresh tokens long-lived. Authorization checks user roles/permissions after authentication.
rag_ground_truths.G3_Deploying a Node.js app to cloud with: Docker packages apps into containers with a Dockerfile specifying the base image, working directory, dependency installation, and start command. docker build creates an image; docker run starts a container. Cloud platforms (AWS ECS, GCP Cloud Run, Heroku) pull images from registries (Docker Hub, ECR). Environment variables are passed at runtime, not baked into images.
rag_ground_truths.G4_Project lifecycle phases: initiation,: The project lifecycle consists of five phases following the PMBOK framework: Initiation (defining scope, objectives, and feasibility in a project charter and identifying key stakeholders), Planning (developing a detailed project management plan covering schedule, budget, resources, risk, and communications), Execution (coordinating people and resources to carry out the plan), Monitoring and Controlling (tracking progress against baselines, managing change requests, and reporting status), and Closure (formally completing the project, obtaining stakeholder sign-off, releasing resources, and capturing lessons learned).
rag_ground_truths.G4_Agile and Scrum: sprints, ceremonies,: Agile is an iterative project management philosophy that delivers value in short cycles called sprints (typically 1-4 weeks). Scrum is the most widely used Agile framework. Key Scrum roles are the Product Owner (owns and prioritizes the product backlog), Scrum Master (facilitates the process and removes impediments), and Development Team. Core ceremonies include Sprint Planning, Daily Stand-up, Sprint Review, and Sprint Retrospective. The product backlog is a prioritized list of user stories; the sprint backlog contains items selected for the current sprint. Kanban is an alternative Agile approach using a visual board to manage continuous flow of work without fixed sprints.
rag_ground_truths.G4_Stakeholder management, risk assessme: Stakeholder management involves identifying all parties affected by the project (stakeholder register), analyzing their influence and interest using a power-interest grid, and planning communications accordingly. Risk management includes identifying risks, assessing probability and impact in a risk register, and defining response strategies (avoid, mitigate, transfer, accept). Project scheduling tools include Gantt charts (bar charts showing tasks against a timeline), Critical Path Method (CPM, identifying the longest sequence of dependent tasks that determines project duration), and Work Breakdown Structure (WBS, decomposing scope into deliverable-oriented components). Common PM tools include MS Project, Smartsheet, Jira, and Asana.
